claims.evidence_text: With typical root distribution, total root length reaches 31 km/m2 by anthesis and maximum rooting depth reaches 1.5-2 m. Over 70% of root length is found in the top 30 cm of the soil.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat maximum rooting depth reaches 1.5-2 m by anthesis (value in meters); exceeds 1 m by GS31. Over 70% of root length is in the top 30 cm.

claims.evidence_text: High rates of uptake of the less mobile nutrients, such as phosphorus (P), only occur when root length densities (RLDs) exceed 5 cm/cm3 of soil. Lower root length densities are adequate for potassium (K) uptake and about 1 cm/cm3 is needed for the take-up of most of the available water
claims.claim_summary: RLD must exceed 5 cm/cm3 for high uptake of less-mobile nutrients (P); ~1 cm/cm3 suffices for water and nitrogen uptake (value in cm/cm3).

claims.evidence_text: By growth stage 31 in winter wheat, maximum rooting depth can exceed 1 m and root dry weight is about 27% of shoot dry weight.
claims.claim_summary: At GS31, winter wheat root dry weight is about 27% of shoot dry weight.

claims.evidence_text: Just 6-11 mm of rain in a day reduces soil surface strength and significantly increases the risk of root lodging. Root systems with greater spread (depth and width) are less prone to root lodging.
claims.claim_summary: Only 6-11 mm rain in a day reduces soil surface strength and significantly increases root lodging risk; wider/deeper root systems are less prone to lodging.

claims.evidence_text: Roots begin to grow at germination, with three to six main (seminal) roots emerging before the second leaf appears.
claims.claim_summary: Three to six main (seminal) roots emerge at germination before the second leaf appears in wheat.

claims.evidence_text: With minimal tillage, enhanced earthworm activity creates long continuous pores in the subsoil to aid root penetration... deep rooting can be self-sustaining, unless wheelings or cultivations destroy soil structure.
claims.claim_summary: Minimal tillage enhances earthworm activity and creates subsoil pores aiding wheat root penetration; cultivation/wheelings that destroy soil structure impair deep rooting.

claims.evidence_text: In winter wheat at growth stage 61, root dry weight is 1.0 t/ha but twice as much assimilate may have been used in root growth.
claims.claim_summary: Winter wheat root dry weight is about 1.0 t/ha at GS61 (anthesis); value reported in t/ha.
